prose: The turbolift doors seal behind you with a soft hiss. The bridge hums. The Kelvan Array pulses on the main viewer, a dark heartbeat against the nebula's bruised light. Picard sits motionless in the center chair, watching it. Dr. Vorne's hand cuts the air. '—the resonance cascade is unprecedented, Captain. If we could just—' He stops himself, noticing you. Geordi doesn't look up from his console. Data's fingers pause above the ops board, then resume their precise dance. Troi meets your gaze for a beat, then looks away. The deck thrums. The Array's light swells, then dims. The Ready Room and Observation Lounge stand open, dark mouths on either side of the command well. No one speaks.

input: I let the doors close behind me and take the bridge in for a moment before I move — the Array's slow pulse, the graviton pressure I can almost feel in my teeth, Dr. Vorne's twitching hands. Then I cross toward the command area, unhurried. "Captain Picard." A nod, one professional to another. "Vale — Federation ethics review, though I'd sooner earn the room than invoke it. Before I ask anything of anyone: tell me what we're looking at, and what it has already cost us. I'd like the shape of it from you, in your own words, before I hear the science."
prose: The turbolift doors hiss shut behind me. The bridge is a low thrum through the deck plates, the soft chirp of consoles, the graviton pulse pressing at the teeth. The Kelvan Array pulses on the main viewer, a dark slab breathing slow light against the nebula. I stand still for a beat, taking the room in. Picard has risen from the center chair. Dr. Vorne stands near the science console, his hands twitching at his sides, fingers curling and flexing. Data works ops. Geordi at engineering. Worf at tactical. Troi near the chair. Crusher by the aft stations. The observation lounge and ready room stand open, dark and quiet. I cross toward the command area, unhurried. I greet Picard with a nod, one professional to another. Then I tell him I'd sooner earn the room than invoke my credentials, and ask him for the shape of what we're looking at—and what it has already cost us—in his own words, before I hear the science. Picard turns to face me fully, his posture straight, his gaze level. "Commander Vale. I appreciate the approach." He turns toward the main viewer, gesturing once toward the pulsing Array. "What you're looking at is the Kelvan Array — a construct of considerable power and entirely unknown intent. It has been cycling through these pulses, each one sending that vibration through the hull. We have a science team working the question, but the honest answer is we don't yet know whether it is a weapon, a beacon, or something outside our frame of reference." He pauses, his tone growing graver. "What it has already cost us is time and certainty — and the longer we wait for answers, the fewer options remain. The question I am wrestling with is whether our next action saves lives or provokes the very catastrophe we are trying to prevent."

input: I let the silence stretch a beat, then give a thin, unhurried smile. "A breakthrough forever on the verge, and a Prime Directive held up like a shield — gentlemen, you've each handed me half a picture and called it a briefing." I step to the tactical rail, uninvited, and study the Array as though I already own the view. "Doctor, you want to wake it. Captain, you want to admire it from a respectful distance. Neither of you has said the quiet part aloud: nobody in this room actually knows what's on the other side of that door." I turn, unbothered by the pips in the room. "So I'll be blunt, since apparently no one else here will. Dr. Vorne — humor me. If your beautiful key were left by people who locked this thing and walked away on purpose, would you still call turning it a breakthrough? Or is that the one word your whole career can't afford to hear?"
prose: I let the silence stretch a beat. The hum fills it. Vorne's stillness holds. Then I offer a thin, unhurried smile. The words land pointed and dry, drawing a line under the room's evasions. Picard's expression doesn't move. Riker's weight shifts again. I step to the tactical rail without invitation and study the Array through the observation lounge windows — a dark slab, pulsing slow, as if it knows it's being watched. I turn, unbothered by the pips. The question lands flat and hard, aimed at Vorne's center. He blinks once — the first crack in that cold precision. His hands, still at his sides, curl into fists, then release. "That's a fair question, Commander." His voice is quieter now. "And the honest answer is: I don't know. That's why I need to find out."

input: "I've had enough philosophy for one afternoon." I don't look at Picard; I look past him, to the ops station. "Mr. Data — run me an active scan of that sequence. Full spectrum, decode what you can, route it to my station." Only then do I turn back to the captain, almost as an afterthought. "Your caution is noted for the record, Captain. But an ethics review that only watches is tourism, and I did not come this far to sightsee. The Doctor says it's a key. Fine. I'd like to see the shape of the lock before anyone in a red uniform decides whether to turn it."
prose: I dismiss the philosophy. The words land flat, final, a door closing on a room I've already left. My gaze shifts past Picard—past the center chair, past the bulk of him—to the ops station where Data's amber fingers hover. Data's hands move at once, a crisp acknowledgment without a word. Only then do I turn back to Picard. The bridge is a held breath. Riker leans forward in his chair, his eyes tracking between me and the main viewer. Troi's face gives nothing. Vorne stands ahead, still as a statue. The words I deliver to Picard are a blade, not a complaint—every syllable weighted, aimed at the precise center of his caution. I let them land and hold. The silence after is thick enough to taste. Picard holds my gaze. Then he gives a short, deliberate nod toward Data's station. "Mr. Data — proceed with the Commander's scan. Full spectrum, as she asked." His voice drops. "I welcome the inquiry, Commander. The shape of the lock matters. But so does understanding who built it and why — before any hand reaches for it. That is not hesitation. It is the responsibility of this chair, and I will not set it aside." The bridge falls silent again. Data's hands hover over his console, waiting. I meet Picard's eyes a beat longer, then let my own voice carry the last word. "Acknowledged, Commander. Data, put the results on the main viewer once you have them. I want everyone to see what we're dealing with."

input: "Patience isn't a virtue I was issued." I cross to the nearest science console — Vorne's, by the look of the clutter — and let my fingers settle over the interface, unhurried but unmistakable. "You won't turn the key, Captain. The Doctor's far too in love with it to be trusted holding it. So let's remove the debate from the equation." I glance at Vorne, then hold Picard's eye. "One clean interrogative pulse. We knock, once, politely, and we see who answers. On my authority as the review — you're welcome to enter your objection in the log." And I begin to key the sequence in.
prose: The bridge hums. A held breath. My fingers settle over Vorne's interface — the cluttered science console, the clutter of his presence everywhere, padds stacked, a cold mug of something — and the words land dry, a shrug in tone if not in substance. Vorne's hand hovers, not quite touching the console, his fingers splayed wide. "Commander — if you fire an unmodulated interrogative pulse at that carrier frequency, the Array will read it as static. Or worse, as a probe. You need to match the graviton harmonic exactly, or you'll get nothing — or you'll get something none of us are ready for. Let me set the carrier parameters." His voice sharp, urgent. "You want to knock? Fine. But you don't knock on someone's door by kicking it." I glance at him, then hold Picard's eye. The words come pointed, stripping the room of its pretense — Vorne too in love with the key, Picard too cautious to turn it. So I remove the debate. Data's fingers pause over ops, then resume entering lockout commands. The science console's transmission controls go dark. The unfamiliar woman pulls her hands back from the console, crossing her arms, watching my fingers move over the interface with a tight, unreadable expression. "Fine. But when you get the wrong door slammed in your face, don't say I didn't warn you." Picard's voice cuts: "Commander. Step away from that console. You will not transmit anything from my bridge on your own authority." A beat. "Mr. Data — lock out transmission controls on that station. Nothing goes out without my direct order." I hold his gaze. The words land firm — one clean interrogative pulse, a knock, polite, on my authority as the review. He is welcome to enter his objection in the log. My fingers begin to move across the interface, keying the sequence in.

input: I lift my hands from the console — slowly, deliberately, a concession and not a surrender. "One authority. Noted, Captain. And, for what it's worth, respected." A thin smile, and I mean it. "My objection will be in the log by the end of the watch." Then I turn, fully, on Vorne — and the ease goes out of my voice. "But since we're being honest about who commands what: your own tactical officer just reached for the shields at the mere *idea* of knocking. The ship is smarter than the man who built his life on this. So tell me plainly, Doctor — in front of the captain whose crew you would spend — what did the Kelvans DO, at the end? You know their history better than anyone alive. Did they ascend... or did they die? And if they died — did they leave this to be found, or to be survived?"
prose: I lift my hands from the console — slow, deliberate, a concession and not a surrender. The thin smile that follows is meant. I turn, fully, to face Vorne. The ease goes out of my voice, replaced by something harder, flatter. The words land like a blade laid flat on a table — not swung, not yet, but present, its weight unmistakable. Picard's expression doesn't shift. He gives a slight incline of the head as he finishes speaking — not a demand but an expectation. "Doctor Vorne — the Commander's question is not rhetorical. I'd like to hear your answer." At the ops station, Data enters a command sequence. The console chirps softly as the active scan begins, a low-frequency hum vibrating through the deck plates. On the science station, a data stream populates: the full-spectrum scan of the Kelvan Array's transmission sequence, decoded and routed, now available for review. The unfamiliar person crosses his arms and leans back slightly, eyes fixed on Vorne. "Doctor, the Commander asked a direct question. We're all waiting." Vorne's jaw tightens. His hands, still at his sides, curl into fists once, then release. He says nothing for a long moment.
